nodes.statement: UK household energy bills are 35% higher than pre-crisis levels with the Q3 2025 price cap at £1,720, and the UK has the second-highest domestic electricity prices among IEA countries
Clean sources currently provide approximately 64% of UK electricity generation, with carbon intensity at 154g CO2/kWh against a net zero requirement to fall below 50g by 2050
UK CfD auction prices show solar at £61/MWh, onshore wind at £64, and offshore wind at £73, representing a 68% fall from AR1 to AR4, with subsidy-free projects now in the pipeline
Hinkley Point C has a strike price of £92.50/MWh in 2012 prices, while system integration costs for renewables add $25-30/MWh at high penetration levels
UK shale gas resource estimates were revised down 83-94%, both fracking attempts caused seismic events requiring suspension, and only 17% of the public supports fracking
The IEA's Net Zero by 2050 pathway states no new oil and gas fields should be approved for development, a position supported by the IPCC, CCC, and Nature
99.9% of peer-reviewed scientific papers agree on human-caused climate change, and the IPCC AR6 states it is unequivocal — this is not contested science
Renewables employed approximately 247,000 people in the UK in 2023 and growing, while oil and gas employ approximately 200,000 and declining

Germany's nuclear phaseout resulted in electricity prices doubling, CO2 falling only 2% per year, and an estimated cost to consumers of $12 billion per year while emission-free generation would have been 94% instead of 61%
France built 56 standardised reactors from 1974, achieving approximately 55g CO2/kWh electricity and exporting approximately €3 billion per year in electricity
NESO estimates £40 billion per year investment is needed for grid decarbonisation at 4x the current rate, requiring 1,000km of new power lines and offshore wind growing from 15GW to 45GW by 2030
CfD generators paid back £346 million to consumers during the 2022 energy crisis, demonstrating the CfD mechanism acts as consumer price insurance during market spikes
Locational marginal pricing could save £30 billion by 2035 according to the Grantham Institute, by pricing electricity to reflect where it is generated and consumed
The Green Homes Grant delivered only 10% of target installations due to contractor shortages and scheme complexity and was found to be poor value for money by the NAO
Denmark achieved approximately 60% of electricity from wind through 50 years of cross-party consensus, community ownership, and integrated industrial strategy creating a world-leading export industry
Renewables are unambiguously cheaper than nuclear power for UK electricity generation
nodes.verdict: INCOMPLETE. Per-unit LCOE favours renewables. But system integration costs add $25-30/MWh at high penetration. IEA/NEA finds nuclear is lowest-cost dispatchable low-carbon technology. Extending existing nuclear is the cheapest electricity of any kind. Both are needed.

nodes.statement: The UK electricity grid can be fully decarbonised by 2030
nodes.verdict: ACHIEVABLE BUT HUGE CHALLENGE. NESO says it requires 'perfect execution'. Offshore wind must triple in 5 years at current build rate of <2GW/yr. Aurora modelling suggests 100% clean by 2051 at current pace. 2030-2035 is realistic with acceleration.

nodes.statement: Fracking is a viable energy source for the UK
nodes.verdict: NOT VIABLE. Resources revised down 83-94%. Both attempts caused seismic events. BGS says earthquake prediction remains a challenge. Expert consensus is UK geology is unsuitable. Only 17% public support.

nodes.statement: Renewable energy in the UK is dependent on subsidies and would collapse without them
nodes.verdict: DECLINING AND APPROACHING SUBSIDY-FREE. CfD prices fell 68%. Older expensive schemes expiring. ~4GW subsidy-free pipeline exists. CfDs also protect consumers in spikes — paid back £346m in 2022. Scrapping subsidies would raise bills not lower them.

nodes.statement: New North Sea oil and gas extraction is compatible with the UK's net zero commitments
nodes.verdict: INCOMPATIBLE. IEA net zero pathway: no new fields. IPCC, CCC, Nature agree. CCC: UK reserves too small to impact consumer prices. UK gas trades internationally — domestic production doesn't stay domestic.

nodes.statement: Reducing CO2 emissions cannot help address climate change
nodes.verdict: FALSE. 99.9% scientific consensus on human-caused climate change. IPCC AR6: 'unequivocal'. This is not contested science. Reform UK's core premise is factually wrong.

nodes.statement: The net employment impact of the energy transition is negative for the UK
nodes.verdict: NET POSITIVE IN AGGREGATE but geographic and skill mismatch is real. Renewables 247k and growing vs oil/gas 200k and declining. No party has a credible place-based transition plan.

nodes.statement: The UK's marginal pricing system means consumers pay gas prices for wind power, and gas sets the electricity price approximately 80% of the time despite providing only 25% of generation

The energy system that simultaneously solves cost, security, and emissions is one that generates most electricity domestically from zero-fuel-cost, zero-carbon sources — the question is pace and mix, not direction
Closing nuclear while decarbonising is the most expensive and least effective approach, as demonstrated by Germany's $12bn/yr cost and minimal emissions reduction
Insulation is the cheapest generation available because energy not used is energy not generated, and every previous voucher-based scheme has failed — only institutional delivery at scale works
Accelerate offshore wind to 40-50GW by early 2030s through reformed planning and CfD auctions, and remove all planning restrictions on onshore wind and solar immediately
Maintain all existing nuclear plants to end of safe operational life with extensions where possible, and pursue standardised SMRs only rather than bespoke large reactors
Issue no new fossil fuel exploration licences while existing production continues during transition, and ban fracking permanently based on UK geological unsuitability
Prioritise grid investment as the single highest infrastructure priority, building 1,000km of new transmission by 2030 and introducing locational marginal pricing saving an estimated £30bn by 2035
Reform wholesale electricity pricing to decouple from gas, either via a single-buyer model or accelerated CfD coverage, saving households an estimated £200 per year
Require all new wind, solar, and transmission projects to offer mandatory community benefit including discounted local electricity and a 5-10% community ownership stake
Create a permanent National Insulation Service as an institutional delivery body insulating 1 million homes per year targeting worst-performing stock, at minimum £3 billion per year investment
Move gas generation capacity into a strategic reserve dispatched only by NESO when renewables cannot meet demand, paying availability fees rather than market prices to remove gas from price-setting

Maintain net zero 2050 pragmatically with annual North Sea licensing, new gas plants, two SMR fleets, treble offshore wind but restrict onshore wind and solar, reduce green levies, and require parliamentary votes before new carbon targets

Achieve clean power by 2030, create Great British Energy as a public company, double onshore wind, triple solar, quadruple offshore wind, no new North Sea licences, windfall tax on oil and gas, and double home insulation funding

Achieve net zero by 2045, invest in renewables and nuclear, launch an emergency home insulation programme, ban fracking and new coal, promote community energy, and invest in tidal, wave, hydrogen, pumped storage and battery technologies

Achieve carbon neutrality before 2040, phase out nuclear entirely, generate 70% from wind by 2030, cancel existing fossil fuel licences, invest £40 billion per year funded by wealth taxes, and take an insulation-first community-owned approach

Scrap net zero entirely along with all renewable subsidies, fast-track North Sea oil and gas, enable fracking, fast-track SMRs, scrap VAT on energy, explore coal mining, and claim CO2 reduction cannot help climate change
